volumeTitle: Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives
label: The Pot of Gold (Section 4.4.638 - Section 4.10.732b)

sections.label: Section 4.4.638
sections.original: Pone hoc sis , aufer cavillam , non ego nunc nugas ago .
sections.translation: Put that down , please ; cease your quibbling ; I ’ m not trifling now .

sections.label: Section 4.4.639
sections.original: Quid ego ponam ? quin tu eloquere quidquid est suo nomine .
sections.translation: What am I to put down ? Why don ’ t you mention it , whatever it is , by its own name ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.640
sections.original: non hercle equidem quicquam sumpsi nec tetigi .
sections.translation: By my faith , I really have neither taken nor touched anything .

sections.label: Section 4.4.640b
sections.original: Ostende huc manus .
sections.translation: Show me your hands , here .

sections.label: Section 4.4.641
sections.original: Em tibi , ostendi , eccas .
sections.translation: Well , I do show them ; see , here they are .

sections.label: Section 4.4.641b
sections.original: Video . age ostende etiam tertiam .
sections.translation: I see them . Come , show me the third [*] , as well .

sections.label: Section 4.4.642
sections.original: Laruae hunc atque intemperiae insaniaeque agitant senem .
sections.translation: Sprites , and frenzy , and madness , possess this old fellow . Are you doing me an injustice , or not ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.643b
sections.original: Fateor , quia non pendes , maximam .
sections.translation: A very great one , I confess , inasmuch as you are not strung up ; and that too shall be done this moment , un less you do confess .

sections.label: Section 4.4.644b
sections.original: Quid fatear tibi ?
sections.translation: What am I to confess to you ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.645
sections.original: Quid abstulisti hinc ?
sections.translation: What it was you took away hence .

sections.label: Section 4.4.645b
sections.original: Di me perdant . si ego tui quicquam abstuli
sections.translation: May the Gods confound me , if I ’ ve taken away anything of yours , ( aside ) and if I don ’ t wish I had taken it away .

sections.label: Section 4.4.646b
sections.original: Agedum , excutedum pallium .
sections.translation: Come then , shake out your cloak .

sections.label: Section 4.4.647
sections.original: Tuo arbitratu .
sections.translation: At your pleasure .

sections.label: Section 4.4.647b
sections.original: Ne inter tunicas habeas .
sections.translation: You haven ’ t it among your under-clothing ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.647c
sections.original: Tempta qua lubet .
sections.translation: Search where you please .

sections.label: Section 4.4.648
sections.original: Vah , scelestus quam benigne : ut ne abstulisse intellegam .
sections.translation: Pshaw ! how civilly the rascal speaks , that I mayn ’ t suppose he has taken it away ! I know your tricks . Come , show me here again that right hand .

sections.label: Section 4.4.650b
sections.original: Em .
sections.translation: Here it is .

sections.label: Section 4.4.650c
sections.original: Nunc laevam ostende .
sections.translation: Now show me your left .

sections.label: Section 4.4.650d
sections.original: Quin equidem ambas profero .
sections.translation: Well , then , I show you both , in fact .

sections.label: Section 4.4.651
sections.original: Iam scrutari mitto . redde huc .
sections.translation: Now I leave off searching . Give back that here .

sections.label: Section 4.4.651b
sections.original: Quid reddam ?
sections.translation: Give back what ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.651c
sections.original: A , nugas agis ,
sections.translation: Are you trifling with me ? You certainly have got it .

sections.label: Section 4.4.652b
sections.original: Habeo ego ? quid habeo ?
sections.translation: I , got it ? Got what ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.652c
sections.original: Non dico , audire expetis .
sections.translation: I shan ’ t say ; you want to hear . Whatever you have of mine , give it back .

sections.label: Section 4.4.653b
sections.original: Insanis : perscrutatus es
sections.translation: You are mad ; you ’ ve searched me all over at your own pleasure , and yet you ’ ve found nothing of yours in my possession .

sections.label: Section 4.4.655
sections.original: Máne , mane . quís illic est ? quis hic íntus alter érat tecum simul ?
sections.translation: Stop , stop ; who was that ? Who was the other [*] that was within here , together with yourself ? Troth , I ’ m undone ; he ’ s now rummaging about within . If I let this one go , he ’ ll escape . At last , I ’ ve now searched this one all over ; he has got nothing . Be off where you please ;

sections.label: Section 4.4.658
sections.original: Iuppiter te dique perdant .
sections.translation: Jupiter and the Gods confound you !

sections.label: Section 4.4.658b
sections.original: Haud male egit gratias .
sections.translation: He returns his thanks not amiss [*] .

sections.label: Section 4.4.659
sections.original: ibo intro atque illi socienno tuo iam interstringam gulam .
sections.translation: I ’ ll go in here now , and I ’ ll at once throttle this accomplice of yours . Will you not fly hence from my sight ? Will you away from here , or no ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.660b
sections.original: Abeo .
sections.translation: I ’ m off .

sections.label: Section 4.4.660c
sections.original: Cave sis † te videam .—
sections.translation: Take you care , please , how I see you .

sections.label: Section 4.5.661
sections.original: Emortuom ego me mavelim leto malo
sections.translation: I would rather that I were dead outright , by a shocking death , than not lay an ambush this day for that old fellow . But he ’ ll not venture now to hide his gold here ; he ’ ll now be carrying it with him , I guess , and be changing the spot .

sections.label: Section 4.5.665
sections.original: attat , foris crépuit . senex eccum aurum ecfert foras .
sections.translation: But hark ! there ’ s a noise at the door . ( Looking in the direction of the Temple .) See , the old fellow ’ s bringing out the gold with him ! Meanwhile , I ’ ll step aside here to the door .

sections.label: Section 4.6.667
sections.original: Fide censebam maxumam multo fidem
sections.translation: I had thought that there was the very greatest dependence upon Faith ; very nearly had she played me a pretty trick [*] . If the raven hadn ’ t come to my assistance , to my sorrow I should have been undone .

sections.label: Section 4.6.670
sections.original: nimis hercle ego illum corvom ad me veniat velim ,
sections.translation: Troth , I very much wish that raven would come to me which gave me the warning , that I might say something kind to him ; for I would as soon give [*] him something to eat as lose it . Now I ’ m thinking of a lonely spot where I shall hide this . The grove of Sylvanus , outside of the wall , is unfrequented ,

sections.label: Section 4.6.675
sections.original: crebro salicto oppletus . ibi sumam locum .
sections.translation: and planted with many a willow ; there will I choose a spot . I ’ m determined to trust Sylvanus [*] , rather than Faith .

sections.label: Section 4.6.677
sections.original: Euge , euge , di me salvom et servatum volunt .
sections.translation: Capital ! capital ! the Gods will me to be safe and preserved ! Now will I run before to that place , and climb up into some tree , and thence will I watch where the old fellow hides the gold .

sections.label: Section 4.6.680
sections.original: quamquam hic manere mé erus sese iusserat ;
sections.translation: Although my master bade me remain here , I ’ m resolved rather to risk a mishap along with emolument .

sections.label: Section 4.7.682
sections.original: Dixi tibi , mater , iuxta rem mecum tenes ,
sections.translation: I ’ ve told you all , mother ; as well as I do myself , you understand all about the daughter of Euclio . Now , I do entreat you , my mother , make mention of it to my uncle , and I now unask of you , mother , that which before I entreated of you , to conceal this from Megadorus .

sections.label: Section 4.7.686
sections.original: Scis tute facta velle me quae tu velis ,
sections.translation: You know , yourself , that what you desire to be done , I desire , and I trust that I shall obtain this of my brother ; and the reason is good , if ’ tis so as you say , that in a drunken fit you debauched this damsel .

sections.label: Section 4.7.690
sections.original: Egone ut te advorsum mentiar , mater mea ?
sections.translation: Could I , my mother , tell a falsehood in your presence ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.691
sections.original: Perii , mea nutrix . obsecro te , uterum dolet .
sections.translation: I die , my nurse ; my pangs are coming on ! I entreat thee for thy protection , Juno Lucina [*] !

sections.label: Section 4.7.692b
sections.original: Em , mater mea ,
sections.translation: Ah ! my mother , I see a more convincing proof for you ; she ’ s crying aloud — she ’ s in the pangs of labour .

sections.label: Section 4.7.694
sections.original: Ei hac intro mecum , gnate mi , ad fratrem meum ,
sections.translation: Come in-doors here , with me , my son , to my brother ,

sections.label: Section 4.7.695
sections.original: ut istúc quod me oras impetratum ab eo auferam .—
sections.translation: that I may obtain a grant from him of that which you beg of me .

sections.label: Section 4.7.696
sections.original: I , iam sequor te , mater . sed servom meum
sections.translation: Go ; I ’ ll follow you this instant , mother . ( EUNOMIA goes into the house .) But my servant , Strobilus , I wonder where he is , whom I ordered to wait here for me . Now I reflect with myself , if he ’ s lending me his assistance , it isn ’ t fair that I should be angry with him .

sections.label: Section 4.7.700
sections.original: ibo intro , ubí de capite meo sunt comitia .—
sections.translation: I ’ ll go in-doors , where they are sitting in judgment [*] upon my life .

sections.label: Section 4.8.701
sections.original: Picis divitiis , qui aureos montes colunt ,
sections.translation: I , by myself , exceed the riches of the Griffins [*] , who inhabit the golden mountains , For I ’ m unwilling to make mention of those other kings , beggarly fellows — I am the king Philip . O charming day !

sections.label: Section 4.8.705
sections.original: nam ut dudum hinc abii , multo illo adveni prior
sections.translation: for when I went from here , just now , I arrived there much the first , and , long before , I placed myself in a tree , and thence observed where the old fellow hid the gold . When he departed thence , I let myself down from the tree , and dug up the pot full of gold . Thence , from that spot ,

sections.label: Section 4.8.710
sections.original: video recipere se senem ; ille me non videt ,
sections.translation: I saw the old fellow betaking himself back again ; he didn ’ t see me , for I turned a little on one side , out of the path . Heyday ! here he comes himself . I ’ ll go and hide this away , at home .

sections.label: Section 4.9.713
sections.original: Perii ínterii occidi . quó curram ? quo nón curram ? tene , téne . quem ? quis ?
sections.translation: I ’ m ruined ! I ’ m done for ! I ’ m murdered ! Whither shall I run ? Whither not run ? Stop him — stop him . Whom ? who ? I don ’ t know . I see nothing ! I ’ m going blindfold ; and , in fact , whither I am going , or where I am , or who I am ,

sections.label: Section 4.9.715
sections.original: nequeó cum animo certum ínvestigare . óbsecro vos ego , mi aúxilio ,
sections.translation: I can ’ t in my mind find out for certain . ( To the AUDIENCE .) I beseech you , give me your aid ( I beg and entreat of you ), and point me out the person that has taken it away . What ’ s the matter ? Why do you laugh ? I ’ m acquainted with you all ; I know that there are many thieves here , who conceal themselves with white clothes and chalk [*] , and sit as though they were honest ! ( To one of the SPECTATORS .) What say you ? You I ’ m resolved to believe ; for I perceive , even by your looks , that you are honest .

sections.label: Section 4.9.720
sections.original: hem , némo habet horum ? occídisti . dic ígitur , quis habet ? néscis ?
sections.translation: Well then , none of these has got it ? You ’ ve been the death of me ! Tell me , then , who has got it ? You don ’ t know ? Oh , wretched , wretched me ! I ’ m done for ! wofully undone ! In most sorry plight I go ; so much groaning , and misfortune , and sorrow , has this day brought upon me , hunger and poverty , too . I ’ m the most utterly ruined of all men upon the earth ! For what need of life have I , who have

sections.label: Section 4.9.725
sections.original: animúmque meum geniúmque meum ; nunc eó alii laetíficantur
sections.translation: lost so much gold that I so carefully watched ? I pinched myself , and my inclinations , and my very heart [*] ! Now others are rejoicing at this , my loss and my misfortune ! I cannot endure it .

sections.label: Section 4.9.727
sections.original: Quínam homo hic ante aédis nostras éiulans conquéritur maerens ?
sections.translation: What person , I wonder , is this before our house lamenting , and that utters complaints with his moaning ? Why , surely , this is Euclio , as I imagine . I ’ m utterly undone ! The thing ’ s all out ; he knows now , as I suppose , that his daughter is brought to bed . I ’ m in a state of uncertainty now

sections.label: Section 4.9.730
sections.original: abeam an maneam , an adeam an fugiam . quid agam édepol nescio .
sections.translation: what I shall do , whether go or remain , accost him or fly .

sections.label: Section 4.10.731
sections.original: Quís homo hic loquitur ?
sections.translation: What person is it that speaks there ?

sections.label: Section 4.10.731b
sections.original: Égo sum miser .
sections.translation: ’ Tis I , wretch that I am .

sections.label: Section 4.10.731c
sections.original: Immo égo sum , et misere pérditus ,
sections.translation: Yes , and so am I , and wretchedly ruined , whose lot is misfortune so great and sorrow .

sections.label: Section 4.10.732b
sections.original: Animo bono es .
sections.translation: Be of good courage .
